title: Improve readability of module exports with consistent JSDoc
Improve readability of optimizeContext with early exit
Extract duplicate metric type arrays into a shared constant
Improve readability of validateContext with early exit
Improve readability of runHealthCheck with early exit
Improve readability with consistent JSDoc and early return
Improve readability of module exports with consistent JSDoc
comment: MARGINAL — Removing 3 lines of JSDoc from ai.ts is a net documentation regression. RSI should not delete existing JSDoc unless it is genuinely redundant or incorrect. The commit message says improve readability but the diff shows deletions only.

title: Improve readability of optimizeContext with early exit
comment: ACCEPTABLE — Adding 2 lines for an early exit guard is a minor but valid improvement. Low risk, low impact. Correct.

title: Extract duplicate prompt patterns into constants
comment: VERY GOOD — PROPOSAL_RESPONSE_FORMAT and EVALUATOR_RESPONSE_FORMAT were duplicated across 5+ domain adapters. Extracting to module-level constants is correct DRY refactoring. Any future schema change only needs one edit. Good catch.

title: Improve readability of detectVramGb with early return
comment: ACCEPTABLE — Restructuring reduces nesting and clarifies the fallback path. However 49 insertions for a readability change is larger scope than ideal. Logic appears preserved. Slightly risky for a single cycle.

title: Add constant-time comparison for API key validation
comment: EXCELLENT — This is a real security fix. Replacing .find() with crypto.timingSafeEqual() eliminates a timing side-channel attack on API key validation. This is exactly the kind of high-value improvement RSI should prioritize. Correct implementation, high impact, no regressions.

title: Improve readability of validateContext with early exit
comment: MARGINAL — A single-line early return guard is a very minor change. Correct but minimal impact. privilegeSeparation.ts is security-critical and deserves more substantive improvements.

title: Improve readability of getPatternContextForFile with early exit
comment: ACCEPTABLE — Guard clause refactoring is correct. Called frequently during RSI cycles so the reduced nesting has some value.

title: Improve readability of runHealthCheck with early exit
comment: GOOD — Inverting the condition for a guard-clause early return reduces nesting and makes the unhealthy path more prominent. scheduleNextCheck() correctly placed in the early return. Good readability improvement.

title: Improve readability with consistent JSDoc and early return
comment: ACCEPTABLE — JSDoc improvement adds useful detail. Guard clause consolidation is minor but correct. However the agent added a second JSDoc block without removing the first — duplicate documentation is a minor quality issue.

title: Extract duplicate metric type arrays into shared constant
comment: GOOD — Extracting repeated metric type arrays into a shared constant is correct DRY refactoring. Reduces maintenance burden when metric types change.

title: Improve readability of analyzeRecurringIssues with consistent error handling
comment: GOOD — Extracting try/catch into handleAnalysisError() wrapper reduces boilerplate and makes error handling consistent across all analyze* functions. Correct behavior preserved.

title: Improve readability of isStale with early exit
comment: ACCEPTABLE — Combining two sequential guard clauses into one with a descriptive comment is a minor but valid readability improvement. Correct, low risk.

title: Improve readability of parseViolations with early exit
comment: GOOD — Early return when contracts.length === 0 avoids iterating test output and building violation arrays when there is nothing to check against. Both a readability and performance improvement. Correct and meaningful.

title: Cache model profiles lookup in recordCost
comment: VERY GOOD — Replacing O(n) .find() with O(1) Map.get() in a hot path called on every LLM invocation is a meaningful performance improvement. Map is module-scoped so initialized once. Correct and impactful.
